Apple___Apple_scab.symptoms: Olive-green to brown velvety spots on leaves and fruit. Leaves may curl and drop early. Fruit develops dark, scabby lesions.
Apple___Apple_scab.treatment: Apply fungicides (captan or myclobutanil) early in the season. Remove and destroy infected leaves. Prune for better air circulation.
Apple___Apple_scab.prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties. Rake and destroy fallen leaves. Apply dormant sprays before bud break.
Apple___Apple_scab.spread: Spreads via fungal spores through wind and rain splash.

Apple___Black_rot.symptoms: Purple spots on leaves that enlarge into brown lesions with purple borders. Fruit shows black rot starting at the calyx end. Cankers appear on branches.
Apple___Black_rot.treatment: Remove mummified fruit and dead wood. Apply fungicides like captan or thiophanate-methyl. Prune infected branches 15cm below visible infection.
Apple___Black_rot.prevention: Maintain tree health with proper nutrition. Remove all mummified fruit before winter. Avoid wounding the tree bark.
Apple___Black_rot.spread: Spores spread through wind and rain, entering through wounds or natural openings.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.symptoms: Bright orange-yellow spots on upper leaf surface in spring. Tube-like structures appear on the leaf underside. Fruit may show similar lesions.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.treatment: Apply fungicides (myclobutanil or mancozeb) from pink bud stage through early summer. Remove nearby juniper/cedar trees if possible.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple varieties. Avoid planting apple trees near eastern red cedars or junipers.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.spread: Requires two hosts — juniper/cedar and apple. Spores travel by wind between them.

Apple___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaf is green, firm, and free of spots, lesions, or discoloration.
Apple___healthy.prevention: Continue regular watering, fertilization, and monitoring. Prune for air circulation annually.

Blueberry___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are glossy green with no spots or lesions.
Blueberry___healthy.prevention: Maintain acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5). Ensure good drainage and regular pruning.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White powdery coating on young leaves, shoots, and fruit. Infected leaves may curl upward. New growth is most vulnerable.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.treatment: Apply sulfur-based or potassium bicarbonate fungicides. Remove heavily infected shoots. Improve air circulation through pruning.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization. Plant in sunny, well-ventilated locations. Choose resistant varieties.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.spread: Airborne spores spread rapidly in warm, dry weather with high humidity nights.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are deep green and smooth.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.prevention: Regular pruning, proper irrigation, and annual monitoring for early pest signs.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.symptoms: Rectangular, tan to gray lesions with distinct parallel edges running between leaf veins. Lesions may merge and kill large leaf areas.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.treatment: Apply foliar fungicides (strobilurins or triazoles) at early disease onset. Rotate crops annually.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.prevention: Use resistant hybrids. Practice crop rotation with non-host crops. Reduce crop debris by tillage.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.spread: Spores spread by wind and rain. Favored by warm, humid conditions and heavy dew.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.symptoms: Small, circular to elongated, brick-red pustules scattered on both leaf surfaces. Pustules rupture to release powdery rust-colored spores.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.treatment: Apply fungicides (triazoles or strobilurins) if infection is severe before tasseling. Early-season infections are most damaging.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.prevention: Plant resistant corn hybrids. Monitor fields regularly during cool, moist weather.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.spread: Wind-borne spores can travel long distances. Thrives in cool temperatures (60–77°F) with high humidity.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Long, elliptical, gray-green to tan lesions (up to 15cm) on leaves. Lesions have a cigar-shaped appearance and may have dark borders.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.treatment: Apply fungicides at early tassel stage if disease appears before silking. Remove crop debris after harvest.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.prevention: Plant resistant hybrids. Rotate crops. Avoid fields with heavy residue from previous corn crops.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.spread: Spores spread by wind and rain splash. Favored by moderate temperatures and prolonged leaf wetness.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are broad, green, and free of lesions.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.prevention: Maintain proper spacing, irrigation, and nutrient management.

Grape___Black_rot.symptoms: Tan to brown circular spots with dark borders on leaves. Infected berries turn brown then shrivel into hard, black mummified fruit.
Grape___Black_rot.treatment: Apply fungicides (mancozeb or myclobutanil) from early shoot growth through veraison. Remove mummified berries immediately.
Grape___Black_rot.prevention: Prune for open canopy. Remove all mummies before spring. Choose resistant varieties.
Grape___Black_rot.spread: Spores released from mummified fruit during wet spring weather. Spread by rain splash.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).symptoms: Tiger-stripe pattern of yellow and brown on leaves. Berries develop dark spots (measles). Sudden vine collapse can occur in hot weather.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).treatment: No effective chemical cure. Remove and destroy infected wood. Apply wound sealants after pruning.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).prevention: Use clean pruning tools. Avoid large pruning wounds. Protect wounds with fungicide paste.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).spread: Fungal spores enter through pruning wounds. Long-term wood disease that develops over years.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.symptoms: Irregular dark brown spots on upper leaf surface, often with a yellow halo. Severe infections cause premature defoliation.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.treatment: Apply copper-based or mancozeb fungicides. Remove infected leaves and improve canopy ventilation.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.prevention: Prune for good air circulation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply preventive fungicide sprays in humid conditions.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.spread: Conidia spread by wind and rain. Thrives in warm, humid climates.

Grape___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are broad, green, and intact.
Grape___healthy.prevention: Regular pruning, canopy management, and monitoring for early disease signs.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).cause: Bacterium (Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus) spread by Asian citrus psyllid
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).symptoms: Blotchy mottling of leaves with asymmetric yellowing. Fruit remains small, misshapen, and bitter. Green, lopsided fruit at harvest.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).treatment: No cure exists. Infected trees should be removed and destroyed. Control psyllid populations with insecticides.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).prevention: Use certified disease-free nursery stock. Monitor and control Asian citrus psyllid populations. Quarantine infected areas.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).spread: Spread by the Asian citrus psyllid insect. One of the most destructive citrus diseases worldwide.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn purple-brown with yellow halos. Spots may fall out leaving shot-holes. Fruit shows sunken, dark lesions.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides during early season. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove infected plant material.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Apply copper sprays during dormancy. Avoid wetting foliage.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.spread: Bacteria spread by rain splash and wind-driven rain. Enter through natural openings and wounds.

Peach___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are lance-shaped, glossy green with no spots.
Peach___healthy.prevention: Annual pruning, proper fertilization, and regular monitoring.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked, greasy-looking spots on leaves that turn brown with yellow halos. Leaves may drop. Fruit shows raised, scabby lesions.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides. Remove infected plant debris. Avoid working in fields when plants are wet.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Rotate crops with non-solanaceous plants. Avoid overhead irrigation.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.spread: Bacteria spread by rain splash, wind, and contaminated tools or hands.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are dark green, smooth, and firm.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.prevention: Maintain proper spacing for air circulation. Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry.

Potato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown to black spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern) on older leaves first. Yellow tissue surrounds the lesions. Severe cases cause defoliation.
Potato___Early_blight.treatment: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil or mancozeb) at first sign of disease. Remove infected leaves promptly.
Potato___Early_blight.prevention: Use certified seed potatoes. Rotate crops. Maintain adequate plant nutrition — stressed plants are more susceptible.
Potato___Early_blight.spread: Spores spread by wind and rain splash. Disease progresses faster in warm, humid conditions.

Potato___Late_blight.symptoms: Water-soaked, pale green lesions on leaf edges that turn brown-black rapidly. White fuzzy growth on leaf undersides in humid conditions. Tubers show reddish-brown rot.
Potato___Late_blight.treatment: Apply fungicides (metalaxyl or chlorothalonil) immediately. Destroy infected plants. Do not compost infected material.
Potato___Late_blight.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Use certified disease-free seed. Monitor fields during cool, wet weather.
Potato___Late_blight.spread: Extremely contagious. Spores spread rapidly by wind and rain. Caused the Irish Potato Famine of the 1840s.

Potato___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are compound, dark green, and free of lesions.
Potato___healthy.prevention: Use certified seed potatoes. Rotate crops every 3–4 years.

Raspberry___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are serrated, green, and free of discoloration.
Raspberry___healthy.prevention: Prune old canes after harvest. Maintain good air circulation.

Soybean___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are trifoliate, medium green, and intact.
Soybean___healthy.prevention: Rotate crops. Monitor fields regularly during growing season.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White, powdery fungal growth on upper and lower leaf surfaces. Infected leaves yellow and die prematurely. Severely affected plants show stunted growth.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.treatment: Apply potassium bicarbonate, neem oil, or sulfur fungicides. Remove heavily infected leaves. Improve air circulation.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid overhead irrigation. Space plants adequately for good airflow.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.spread: Airborne spores spread rapidly in warm days with cool nights and moderate humidity.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.symptoms: Small, irregular purple to red spots on upper leaf surface. Centers turn tan to gray. Severely infected leaves appear scorched and die.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.treatment: Apply fungicides (captan or myclobutanil). Remove and destroy infected leaves. Avoid wetting foliage.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.prevention: Use certified disease-free plants. Provide good drainage. Remove old leaves after harvest.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.spread: Spores spread by rain splash and overhead irrigation. Favored by wet, humid conditions.

Strawberry___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are trifoliate, bright green, and firm.
Strawberry___healthy.prevention: Renovate beds after harvest. Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves, stems, and fruit. Spots turn brown with yellow halos. Fruit develops raised, scabby lesions that reduce marketability.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides. Avoid overhead irrigation. Destroy infected plant material.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Rotate crops. Disinfect tools between plants.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.spread: Spreads by rain splash, wind, and contaminated equipment. Entry through wounds and stomata.

Tomato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric target-board rings on older, lower leaves first. Yellow tissue around lesions. Severe infections cause defoliation from the bottom up.
Tomato___Early_blight.treatment: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil or copper). Remove lower infected leaves. Stake plants to improve air circulation.
Tomato___Early_blight.prevention: Mulch around plants. Avoid wetting leaves. Rotate crops annually.
Tomato___Early_blight.spread: Spores spread by wind and rain splash. Favored by warm temperatures and leaf wetness.

Tomato___Late_blight.symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked gray-green lesions on leaves. White mold on leaf undersides in humid conditions. Dark brown lesions on stems. Fruit rots rapidly.
Tomato___Late_blight.treatment: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil or metalaxyl) immediately. Remove and destroy all infected plants. Do not compost.
Tomato___Late_blight.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid overhead watering. Monitor during cool, wet weather.
Tomato___Late_blight.spread: Highly contagious. Wind-borne spores can devastate entire fields within days.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surface. Olive-green to grayish-brown velvety mold on leaf undersides. Infected leaves curl and drop.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.treatment: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil or mancozeb). Improve greenhouse ventilation. Reduce humidity levels.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.prevention: Use resistant varieties. Maintain relative humidity below 85%. Space plants for air circulation.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.spread: Airborne spores thrive in high humidity and poor ventilation, especially in greenhouses.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Many small circular spots with dark brown borders and tan/gray centers on lower leaves. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in lesion centers. Rapid defoliation follows.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.treatment: Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil or copper). Remove infected leaves. Avoid wetting foliage.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.prevention: Rotate crops. Mulch around plants. Stake tomatoes to keep foliage off ground.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.spread: Spores spread by rain splash and infected soil. Favored by warm, wet conditions.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.symptoms: Fine stippling or bronzing of leaves. Tiny webbing visible on leaf undersides. Leaves turn yellow, dry, and drop. Mites are tiny and hard to see with the naked eye.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.treatment: Apply miticides or insecticidal soap. Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis). Spray plants with water to dislodge mites.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.prevention: Maintain adequate plant moisture. Avoid dusty conditions. Monitor undersides of leaves regularly.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.spread: Spread by wind, clothing, and tools. Populations explode rapidly in hot, dry conditions.

Tomato___Target_Spot.symptoms: Brown circular lesions with concentric rings (target-like) on leaves, stems, and fruit. Lesions may have yellow halos. Affected leaves drop prematurely.
Tomato___Target_Spot.treatment: Apply fungicides (azoxystrobin or chlorothalonil). Remove and destroy infected plant debris.
Tomato___Target_Spot.prevention: Improve plant spacing and air circulation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Rotate crops.
Tomato___Target_Spot.spread: Spores spread by wind and rain. Thrives in warm, humid conditions.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.cause: Virus (Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus — TYLCV) transmitted by whitefly
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.symptoms: Upward curling and yellowing of leaf margins. Stunted plant growth. Flowers drop without setting fruit. Young leaves are small and cupped.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.treatment: No cure for infected plants. Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Control whitefly populations with insecticides or reflective mulch.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.prevention: Use virus-resistant tomato varieties. Control whitefly with yellow sticky traps. Use insect-proof netting in greenhouses.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.spread: Transmitted exclusively by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Not spread by contact.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves. Leaves may be distorted, curled, or fern-like. Fruit shows yellow blotches or internal browning.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.treatment: No cure. Remove and destroy infected plants. Disinfect tools with 10% bleach solution.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.prevention: Use certified virus-free seed. Wash hands before handling plants. Control aphid vectors. Plant resistant varieties.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.spread: Highly contagious. Spreads by contact — hands, tools, clothing. Also seed-borne.

Tomato___healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are deep green, pinnate, and free of spots or discoloration.
Tomato___healthy.prevention: Stake plants for airflow. Use drip irrigation. Rotate crops each season.
